summary: Pulmonary vein distension can occur but isn't universally stated; RV/LV wall normalization doesn't generally happen after significant progression into Eisenmenger physiology; central PA dilatation occurs commonly reflecting pathophysiology changes over time; while peripheral pruned appearance may be seen selectively among other structural alterations indicative of disease evolution towards more severe states characterized by high pressure gradients across shunts leading ultimately toward irreversible lung damage manifest through these specific anatomic findings. The correct answer appears most aligned with option D regarding peripheral pruning given its specificity within evolving hemodynamic profiles associated closely enough clinically for this scenario
